sources.resource_title: Back pain - when you see the doctor
sources.text: Do you have any numbness or tingling ? Any weakness or loss of function in your leg or elsewhere? What makes the pain worse? Lifting, twisting, standing, or sitting for long periods? What makes you feel better? Does bearing down or straining make your pain worse? You will also be asked if you have other symptoms, which may point to a more serious cause. Tell your provider if you have had weight loss, fever, a change in urination or bowel habits, loss of balance, or a history of cancer . Your provider will perform a physical exam to try to find the exact location of your pain, and determine how it affects your movement. Your back will be pressed on at different spots to find where it hurts. You will also be asked to: Sit, stand, and walk Walk on your toes and then your heels Bend forward, backward, and sideways Lift your legs straight up while lying down Move your back in certain positions If the pain is worse and goes down your leg when you lift your legs straight up while lying down, you may have sciatica , especially if you also feel numbness or tingling going down the same leg.

cases.output:
DIAGNOSIS:
You have a lumbar strain. This means you pulled or stretched the muscles and soft tissues in your lower back.

WHAT WE FOUND:
You hurt your lower back after lifting boxes. Your exam in the emergency department was reassuring. You have normal strength and feeling in your legs. You can walk normally. You do not have a fever. You are not having problems controlling your bladder or bowels.

WHAT TO DO AT HOME:
You can move around as much as you can tolerate. Resting in bed for long periods can actually slow your recovery. Try to stay as active as possible without making the pain much worse. Gentle walking is usually helpful. You can use ice or heat on your back, whichever feels better to you. A physical therapist can teach you safe stretching and strengthening exercises when you are ready. You may also consider seeing a chiropractor or massage therapist if your pain continues.

MEDICATIONS:
Continue taking ibuprofen and acetaminophen as needed for pain. You can find these at the store without a prescription. Talk with your doctor before using ibuprofen if you have heart disease, high blood pressure, kidney disease, or have had stomach ulcers or bleeding in your stomach in the past. Do not take more than the amount shown on the bottle.

RETURN TO ED IF:
Come back to the emergency department right away if you develop any of these problems: new weakness in your legs, new numbness between your legs or in your buttocks, trouble controlling your pee or poop, a fever, trouble walking or keeping your balance, severe pain that keeps getting worse, or back pain after a hard fall or injury.

FOLLOW UP:
If your back pain lasts longer than four weeks, follow up with your primary care doctor. Your doctor may refer you to a specialist or physical therapist if needed.

sources.text: As sciatica is a symptom of a more specific medical condition, the underlying cause should be identified and treated. In most cases, no surgical treatment is required and recovery occurs with conservative treatment. Conservative (non-surgical) treatment is best in many cases. Your provider may recommend the following steps to reduce your symptoms: Take over-the-counter pain relievers such as ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin IB) or acetaminophen (Tylenol). Apply heat or ice to the painful area. Try ice for the first 48 to 72 hours, then use heat. Measures to take care of your back at home may include: Bed rest is not recommended. Back exercises are recommended early on to strengthen your back. Start exercising again after 2 to 3 weeks. Include exercises to strengthen your abdominal (core) muscles and improve the flexibility of your spine. Reduce your activity for the first couple of days. Then, slowly start your usual activities. Do not do heavy lifting or twisting of your back for the first 6 weeks after the pain begins.

sources.text: Use proper posture when sitting, standing, or driving. Sit up straight and don't slump. Your provider may prescribe muscle relaxants. This will relax the muscle so you can exercise and stretch it. Injections of steroid medicines into the area may also help. For more severe pain, your provider may recommend electrotherapy such as TENS. This treatment uses electrical stimulation to reduce pain and stop muscle spasms. Also, for more severe pain, your provider may recommend physical therapy focusing on muscle and core strengthening to reduce pain and muscle spasms. As a last resort, your provider may recommend surgery to release the muscle and relieve pressure on the nerve.

sources.text: Sciatica is a symptom of a problem with the sciatic nerve, the largest nerve in the body. It controls muscles in the back of your knee and lower leg and provides feeling to the back of your thigh, part of your lower leg, and the sole of your foot. When you have sciatica, you have pain, weakness, numbness, or tingling. It can start in the lower back and extend down your leg to your calf, foot, or even your toes. It's usually on only one side of your body. Causes of sciatica include: A ruptured intervertebral disk Narrowing of the spinal canal that puts pressure on the nerve, called spinal stenosis An injury such as a pelvic fracture. In many cases no cause can be found. Sometimes sciatica goes away on its own. Treatment, if needed, depends on the cause of the problem. It may include exercises, medicines, and surgery.
sources.preview: Sciatica is a symptom of a problem with the sciatic nerve, the largest nerve in the body. It controls muscles in the back of your knee and lower leg and provides feeling to the back of your thigh, part of your lower leg, and the sole of your foot. When you hav

cases.output:
DIAGNOSIS:
Sciatica. This is pain that travels from your lower back down your leg. It happens when the sciatic nerve gets irritated or pressed on.

WHAT WE FOUND:
You have pain in your lower back that goes down your right leg. The exam showed that your sciatic nerve is irritated. You do not have weakness in your legs. You can control your bladder and bowels normally. You do not have a fever. These are good signs.

WHAT TO DO AT HOME:
Do not stay in bed. Resting in bed does not help sciatica get better. Move around as much as you can handle. Start with light activity and slowly do more each day. Do not lift heavy things or twist your back for the next six weeks. Use heat or ice on the painful area. Try ice for the first two to three days, then switch to heat if that feels better. You can start gentle back exercises after two to three weeks. Focus on exercises that make your stomach muscles stronger and help your back move better. Sit up straight when you sit, stand, or drive. Do not slump.

MEDICATIONS:
You can take ibuprofen or naproxen for pain. These medicines help with pain and swelling. Do not take these medicines if you have kidney disease, stomach ulcers, bleeding problems, take blood thinners, or if a doctor told you to avoid them. You can also take acetaminophen for pain. Follow the directions on the bottle.

RETURN TO ED IF:
Come back to the emergency department right away if you have any of these problems. New weakness in your leg or foot. New numbness in your groin, buttocks, or between your legs. Trouble controlling your pee or poop. Fever. You cannot walk or put weight on your leg. Pain that gets much worse even after taking medicine.

FOLLOW UP:
See your primary care doctor in the next one to two weeks. Your doctor can check how you are doing and help you if the pain is not getting better. Most people with sciatica get better in a few weeks with home care. If your pain lasts longer than four weeks, call your doctor.

cases.output:
DIAGNOSIS:
Low back pain with no injury.

WHAT WE FOUND:
You have low back pain that started two days ago. You did not hurt your back in a fall or accident. Your exam today was normal. Your nerves are working well. You do not need X-rays or other tests today.

WHAT TO DO AT HOME:
Rest your back but do not stay in bed all day. Move around gently. Short walks are good. Try sitting, standing, and lying down in different positions to find what feels best. Use ice packs on your back for 15 minutes at a time during the first few days. After a few days you can try heat if it helps. Do not lift heavy things. Do not twist or bend deeply until your pain gets better. Light stretching may help after a few days. A physical therapist can teach you safe exercises when you are ready.

MEDICATIONS:
You can take pain medicine you buy at the store. Ibuprofen or naproxen can help with pain and swelling. Do not take ibuprofen or naproxen if you have kidney disease, stomach ulcers, stomach bleeding, or take blood thinners. Do not take them if a doctor told you to avoid them. Acetaminophen is another choice for pain. Follow the directions on the bottle. Do not take more than the bottle says.

RETURN TO ED IF:
Come back to the emergency department right away if you have any of these problems. New numbness or tingling in your groin, buttocks, or private area. New weakness in your legs. Trouble controlling your pee or poop. Fever. You fall or get hurt badly. You cannot walk or stand. Your pain gets much worse and does not get better with rest and medicine.

FOLLOW UP:
See your regular doctor in one to two weeks if your pain is not getting better. Your doctor may send you to a back specialist or physical therapist if needed. Most low back pain gets better in a few weeks with rest and pain medicine.

sources.text: You may feel a variety of symptoms if you have hurt your back. You may have a tingling or burning sensation, a dull achy feeling, or sharp pain. The pain may be mild, or it can be so severe that you are unable to move. Depending on the cause of your back pain, you may also have pain in your leg, hip, or the bottom of your foot. You may also have weakness in your legs and feet.

cases.output:
DIAGNOSIS:
Back pain

WHAT WE FOUND:
You have back pain. We checked you today and did not find signs of serious problems. Your exam looked good. Your strength and feeling were normal. You did not have fever or signs of infection.

WHAT TO DO AT HOME:
Rest when you need to, but try to stay gently active. Avoid heavy lifting, twisting, and bending for the next few days. Use ice packs for 15 to 20 minutes at a time during the first two days if it helps. After that, you can try heat if you prefer. Walk short distances several times a day to keep your back from getting stiff. Avoid sitting or standing in one position for a long time.

MEDICATIONS:
You can take over-the-counter pain medicine to help with discomfort. Acetaminophen can help with pain. Ibuprofen or naproxen can help with pain and swelling. Follow the directions on the bottle. Do not take ibuprofen or naproxen if you have stomach ulcers, kidney problems, or if your doctor has told you to avoid these medicines. Take pain medicine with food to protect your stomach.

RETURN TO ED IF:
Come back to the emergency department right away if you have any of these problems:

New weakness in your legs or trouble walking
New numbness or tingling in your legs, feet, or private area
Loss of control of your urine or bowel movements
Fever
Pain after a fall or injury
Pain going down your leg below the knee
Pain so severe you cannot get comfortable in any position
New trouble with balance

FOLLOW UP:
See your regular doctor in one to two weeks if your back pain is not getting better. Call your doctor sooner if your pain gets worse or if you lose weight without trying.

Take it off from time to time to allow the muscles to get stronger.

sources.resource_title: Neck pain or spasms - self care
sources.text: As part of physical therapy, you may receive a massage and stretching exercises along with exercises to strengthen your neck. Exercise can help you: Improve your posture Strengthen your neck and improve flexibility A complete exercise program should include: Stretching and strength training. Follow the instructions of your provider or physical therapist. This may involve walking, riding a stationary bicycle, or swimming. These activities can help improve blood flow to your muscles and promote healing. They also strengthen muscles in your stomach, neck, and back. Stretching and strengthening exercises are important in the long run. Keep in mind that starting these exercises too soon after an injury can make your pain worse. Strengthening the muscles in your upper back can ease the stress on your neck. Your physical therapist can help you determine when to begin neck stretching and strengthening exercises and how to do them.
sources.preview: As part of physical therapy, you may receive a massage and stretching exercises along with exercises to strengthen your neck. Exercise can help you: Improve your posture Strengthen your neck and improve flexibility A complete exercise program should include: S

sources.text: Treatment and self-care for your neck pain depend on the cause of the pain. You will need to learn: How to relieve the pain What your activity level should be What medicines you can take For minor, common causes of neck pain: Take over-the-counter pain relievers such as ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin IB) or acetaminophen (Tylenol). Apply heat or ice to the painful area. Use ice for the first 48 to 72 hours, and then use heat after that. Apply heat with warm showers, warm compresses, or a heating pad. To prevent injury to your skin, DO NOT fall asleep with a heating pad or ice bag in place. Stop normal physical activity for the first few days. This helps calm your symptoms and reduce inflammation. Do slow range-of-motion exercises, up and down, side to side, and from ear to ear. This helps gently stretch the neck muscles. Have a partner gently massage the sore or painful areas.

sources.preview: Contact your provider for an appointment if symptoms do not improve with treatment, or if new symptoms develop. Torticollis that occurs after an injury or with illness may be serious. Seek medical help right away if this occurs.

cases.output:
DIAGNOSIS:
Neck strain from sleeping in an awkward position.

WHAT WE FOUND:
You have strained the muscles in your neck. This happened from sleeping in an awkward position. Your exam today was reassuring. You have no weakness or numbness in your arms. You can move your neck in all directions, even though it is uncomfortable. This type of injury usually gets better with rest and simple home treatments.

WHAT TO DO AT HOME:
Rest your neck for the first few days. Avoid heavy lifting and activities that make the pain worse. After the first two to three days, start gentle neck exercises. Move your head slowly up and down, side to side, and ear to ear. These stretches help your neck muscles loosen up.

Use ice packs on your neck for the first two to three days. Place the ice pack on the sore area for 15 to 20 minutes at a time. After two to three days, switch to heat. Use a heating pad, warm shower, or warm compress. Do not fall asleep with a heating pad or ice pack on your skin.

Sleep on a firm mattress with a pillow that supports your neck. A regular pillow or a special neck pillow can help. Ask someone to gently massage the sore areas if that feels good.

Gentle activities like walking can help your neck heal. They improve blood flow to the injured muscles. As you feel better, you can slowly return to your normal activities.

MEDICATIONS:
You can take over-the-counter pain relievers. Ibuprofen and acetaminophen can help reduce pain and swelling. Do not take ibuprofen if you have kidney problems, stomach ulcers, or are allergic to it. Follow the directions on the bottle.

RETURN TO ED IF:
Come back to the emergency department if you develop any of these symptoms:

New numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand.

Your neck pain gets much worse or is so bad you cannot get comfortable.

You develop a fever with a very stiff neck.

You have new trouble walking or keeping your balance.

You lose control of urination or bowel movements.

You injure your neck again in a fall or accident.

FOLLOW UP:
If your pain does not improve in one week with home care, call your regular doctor for an appointment. Your doctor may recommend physical therapy if the pain continues. Physical therapy can include massage, stretching, and strengthening exercises to help your neck heal completely.

sources.text: Your health care provider may order an x-ray to look for a bone fracture, or an MRI scan to look for an injury to the ligament. To help your ankle heal, your provider may treat you with a brace, a cast, or a splint, and may give you crutches to walk on. You may be asked to place only part or none of your weight on the bad ankle. You will also need to do physical therapy or exercises to help you recover from the injury.

sources.text: You should contact your provider if you notice any of the following: You cannot walk, or walking is very painful. The pain does not get better after ice, rest, and pain medicine. Your ankle does not feel any better after 5 to 7 days. Your ankle continues to feel weak or gives away easily. Your ankle is increasingly discolored (red or black and blue), or it becomes numb or tingly, feels cold, or has changes in sensation.

sources.text: Wear a boot if your provider suggests it. This can protect your foot and allow your ligaments to heal better. Talk to your provider before returning to any high impact activity or sport.

cases.output:
DIAGNOSIS:

You have an ankle sprain. This means you stretched or tore one of the ligaments in your ankle. The x-ray showed no broken bones.

WHAT WE FOUND:

You hurt your ankle while walking. Your ankle has mild swelling. The blood flow and nerves in your foot and ankle are working normally. The x-ray did not show a fracture.

WHAT TO DO AT HOME:

Rest your ankle. Try not to walk on it too much for the first few days. Use crutches if walking hurts.

Put ice on your ankle for 15 to 20 minutes at a time. Do this several times a day for the first 2 to 3 days. Put a towel between the ice and your skin.

Keep your ankle raised above your heart when you sit or lie down. This helps reduce swelling.

Wear the brace we gave you. This protects your ankle while it heals.

Do not walk or exercise too much too soon. Start slowly when your ankle feels better.

MEDICATIONS:

You can take over-the-counter pain medicine like ibuprofen or naproxen to help with pain and swelling. Follow the directions on the bottle. Take these medicines with food. Do not use them if you have stomach ulcers or kidney problems.

You can also use acetaminophen for pain. Do not take more than the bottle says to take.

RETURN TO ED IF:

You cannot walk at all or walking causes very bad pain.

Your ankle becomes very swollen or the swelling gets much worse.

Your foot or ankle becomes numb, tingly, very cold, or changes color to dark red or black and blue.

You develop a fever.

The pain gets much worse instead of better.

FOLLOW UP:

See your regular doctor in 5 to 7 days to check how your ankle is healing. Your doctor can help you know when it is safe to return to sports or exercise. You may need physical therapy if your ankle is not getting better.

sources.text: Therapists will teach you how to properly lift, dress, walk, and perform other activities. They will teach you how to strengthen muscles that help support the spine. You will also learn how to increase flexibility in your spine and legs. Take care of your back at home : Reduce activity for the first few days. Slowly restart your usual activities. Avoid heavy lifting or twisting your back for the first 6 weeks after the pain starts. After 2 to 3 weeks, gradually start exercising again. INJECTIONS Steroid medicine injections into the back in the area of the herniated disk may help control pain for several months. These injections reduce swelling around the spinal nerve and disk and relieve many symptoms. They do not solve the underlying problem and your pain may return after weeks or months. Spinal injections are an outpatient procedure. SURGERY Surgery may be an option if your symptoms do not go away with other treatments and time.

sources.text: Contact your provider right away if you have: Back pain after a severe blow or fall Burning with urination or blood in your urine A history of cancer Loss of control over urine or stool (incontinence) Pain traveling down your legs below the knee Pain that is worse when you lie down Pain that wakes you up at night Redness or swelling on the back or spine Severe pain that does not allow you to get comfortable Unexplained fever with back pain Weakness or numbness in your buttocks, thigh, leg, or pelvis Difficulty walking or maintaining your balance Also contact your provider if: You have been losing weight unintentionally You use steroids or inject drugs You have had back pain before, but this episode is different and feels worse This episode of back pain has lasted longer than 4 weeks

sources.text: Use a stool under your feet while sitting so that your knees are higher than your hips. Place a small pillow or rolled towel behind your lower back while sitting or driving for long periods. If you drive long-distance, stop and walk around every hour. Do not lift heavy objects just after a long ride. Do exercises to strengthen your abdominal muscles. This will strengthen your core to decrease the risk of further injuries. Try methods such as yoga, tai chi, or massage.

cases.output:
DIAGNOSIS:

Herniated disk with recurrent back pain.

WHAT WE FOUND:

You came to the emergency department today with back pain from your known herniated disk. We checked you carefully and found no new weakness in your legs. You told us you have no trouble controlling your bladder or bowel. The pain got better with medication we gave you.

WHAT TO DO AT HOME:

Rest for the first few days but avoid staying in bed all day. Start moving around gently as you feel able. Do not lift heavy objects or twist your back for the next six weeks. After two to three weeks, you can slowly start light exercise again.

When sitting, place a small pillow or rolled towel behind your lower back for support. Try putting a stool under your feet so your knees are higher than your hips. If you stand at work, rest one foot on a stool and switch feet throughout the day.

Apply ice to your back for 15 to 20 minutes at a time during the first two days. After that, you can use heat if it feels better.

MEDICATIONS:

Take over-the-counter pain medicine as needed. Ibuprofen or naproxen can help with pain and swelling. Acetaminophen is another option for pain relief. If you have stomach problems, kidney disease, or take blood thinners, ask your doctor before using ibuprofen or naproxen.

RETURN TO ED IF:

Come back to the emergency department right away if you develop any of these problems:

You lose control of your bladder or bowel.

You notice new numbness, weakness, or tingling in your legs, feet, buttocks, or pelvis.

Your leg becomes so weak you cannot walk or keep your balance.

Your pain becomes severe and you cannot get comfortable in any position.

You develop a fever with your back pain.

Your pain shoots down your leg below the knee.

FOLLOW UP:

See your regular doctor or spine specialist within one to two weeks. They can help you with a long-term treatment plan. This may include physical therapy to strengthen your back and learn safe ways to move, lift, and exercise. Your doctor can also discuss other treatments if your pain does not improve over time.

The injection can be repeated over time if needed.

This can help you continue with your normal activities. Your provider may recommend measures to reduce your pain, including: A back brace to support your back Cold packs and heat therapy Traction Physical therapy, involving stretching and strengthening exercises Counseling to learn ways to understand and manage your pain These other providers may also help: Massage therapist Someone who performs acupuncture Someone who does spinal manipulation (a chiropractor, osteopathic physician, or physical therapist) If needed, your provider may prescribe medicines to help with your back pain: Aspirin, naproxen (Aleve), or ibuprofen (Advil), which you can buy without a prescription Low doses of prescription medicines Narcotics or opioids when the pain is severe If your pain does not improve with medicine, physical therapy, and other treatments, your provider may recommend an epidural injection .

sources.text: To get better quickly, take the right measures when you first feel pain. Here are some tips for how to handle pain: Stop or reduce normal physical activity for the first few days. This will help relieve your symptoms and reduce any swelling in the area of the pain. Apply heat or ice to the painful area. One good method is to use ice for the first 48 to 72 hours and then use heat. Take over-the-counter pain relievers such as ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin) or acetaminophen (Tylenol). Follow package instructions on how much to take. Do not take more than the recommended amount. While sleeping, try lying in a curled-up, fetal position with a pillow between your legs. If you usually sleep on your back, place a pillow or rolled towel under your knees to relieve pressure. A common misbelief about back pain is that you need to rest and avoid activity for a long time.

cases.output:
DIAGNOSIS:

Flare of chronic low back pain.

WHAT WE FOUND:

You came to the emergency department with a flare of your long-term back pain. We checked you and did not find any signs of nerve damage, infection, or injury. Your exam was reassuring.

WHAT TO DO AT HOME:

You can slowly return to your normal activities as you feel able. Staying in bed for a long time can make back pain worse. Start with gentle movement and add more activity each day as your pain allows.

Use heat on your lower back to help with pain and stiffness. You can use a heating pad or warm towel for 15 to 20 minutes at a time. Some people find cold packs helpful in the first few days. Try both and use what works best for you.

If you usually sleep on your back, put a pillow under your knees. If you sleep on your side, try curling up with a pillow between your legs.

MEDICATIONS:

You can take over-the-counter pain medicine like ibuprofen or naproxen if it is safe for you. Do not take ibuprofen or naproxen if you have kidney disease, heart disease, high blood pressure, or have had stomach ulcers or bleeding in your stomach. Follow the directions on the bottle. Do not take more than the amount listed.

You can also use acetaminophen for pain.

RETURN TO ED IF:

Come back to the emergency department right away if you have:

Loss of control of your bladder or bowels.
New numbness or weakness in your legs or feet.
Severe pain that gets much worse even with medicine.
Fever with your back pain.
Pain that spreads down your leg with numbness or tingling.

FOLLOW UP:

See your regular doctor within the next one to two weeks. Your doctor can help you manage your long-term back pain. They may suggest physical therapy, stretching exercises, or other treatments. If your pain does not get better with home care and medicine, your doctor can discuss other options with you.

sources.text: Long car rides and getting in and out of the car can be hard on your back. If you have a long commute to work, consider some of these changes: Adjust your car seat to make it easier to enter, sit in, and get out of your car. Bring your seat as far forward as possible to avoid bending forward when you are driving. If you drive long distances, stop and walk around every hour. Do not lift heavy objects right after a long car ride.

sources.text: After 2 to 3 weeks, you should gradually start exercising again. Begin with light aerobic activity. Walking, riding a stationary bicycle, and swimming are great examples. These activities can improve blood flow to your back and promote healing. They also strengthen muscles in your stomach and back. You may benefit from physical therapy. Your provider will determine whether you need to see a physical therapist and can refer you to one. The physical therapist will first use methods to reduce your pain. Then, the therapist will teach you ways to prevent getting back pain again. Stretching and strengthening exercises are important. But, starting these exercises too soon after an injury can make your pain worse. A physical therapist can tell you when to begin stretching and strengthening exercises and how to do them. If your pain lasts longer than 1 month, your primary provider may refer you to a physiatrist (specialist in rehabilitation), or an orthopedist (bone specialist).

If your pain has not improved after the use of medicines, physical therapy, and other treatments, your provider may recommend an epidural injection . You may also see: A massage therapist Someone who performs acupuncture Someone who does spinal manipulation (a chiropractor, osteopathic doctor, or physical therapist) Sometimes, a few visits to these specialists will help back pain.

sources.text: Keep activity to a minimum. The more time the injury has rest, the better. For support during activity, you can use a sacroiliac belt or lumbar brace. Physical therapy is an important part of the healing process. It will help relieve pain and increase strength. Talk to your provider or physical therapist about exercises to do. Here is an example of an exercise for your lower back: Lie flat on your back with your knees bent and feet flat on the ground. Slowly, begin to rotate your knees to the right side of your body. Stop when you feel pain or discomfort. Slowly rotate back toward the left side of your body until you feel pain. Rest in the starting position. The best way to get rid of SIJ pain is to stick to a care plan. The more you rest, ice, and do exercises, the quicker your symptoms will improve or your injury will heal.

cases.output:
DIAGNOSIS:
Back pain improving after injury

WHAT WE FOUND:
Your exam today looked good. Your back pain is getting better. You are ready to start returning to work, but you need to protect your back while it finishes healing.

WHAT TO DO AT HOME:
Start moving more each day but avoid sudden increases in activity. Walking and gentle movement help your back heal. If you drive to work, bring your car seat forward so you do not bend when driving. Stop every hour on long drives to walk around. Do not lift heavy items right after a long car ride.

When you stand for long periods, rest one foot on a low stool or step. Switch feet every few minutes throughout the day. Take breaks to sit down when you can.

Return to work gradually. Start with light duty if possible. Avoid heavy lifting for the first few days. When you do lift, keep items close to your body. Bend your knees instead of your back. Ask for help with heavy or awkward items.

Ice your back for 15 to 20 minutes if pain increases after activity.

MEDICATIONS:
You can take ibuprofen or naproxen to help with pain and swelling. Take these medicines with food to protect your stomach. Do not use them if you have stomach ulcers or kidney problems.

You can also take acetaminophen for pain.

RETURN TO ED IF:
You lose control of your bowel or bladder.

You develop numbness or weakness in your legs.

You have severe pain that does not improve with rest and medicine.

You develop a fever.

Your pain suddenly gets much worse.

FOLLOW UP:
Follow the work restrictions on your note. If your job requires heavy lifting and your pain is not better in one to two weeks, see your regular doctor to discuss your return to work plan. Your doctor may refer you to physical therapy to help strengthen your back and teach you safe lifting techniques.

cases.condition: heat versus ice for back pain
cases.ed_note: Adult with acute low back pain and no red flags. Asked whether to use ice or heat at home.

sources.text: Your provider may recommend these steps for the first few days or weeks after your injury or when starting treatment for SIJ pain: Rest. Keep activity to a minimum and stop movements or activity that worsen the pain. Ice your lower back or upper buttocks for about 20 minutes 2 to 3 times a day. Do not apply ice directly to the skin. Use a heating pad on the low setting to help loosen tight muscles and relieve soreness. Massage the muscles in the lower back, buttocks, and thigh. Take pain medicines as instructed. For pain, you can use ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin), naproxen (Aleve, Naprosyn), or acetaminophen (Tylenol). You can buy these medicines at the store without a prescription. Talk with your provider before using these medicines if you have heart disease, high blood pressure, kidney disease, or have had stomach ulcers or internal bleeding in the past. Do not take more than the amount recommended on the bottle or by your provider.

sources.resource_title: Taking care of your back at home
sources.text: A common myth about back pain is that you need to rest and avoid activity for a long time. In fact, health care providers do not recommend bed rest. If you have no sign of a serious cause for your back pain (such as loss of bowel or bladder control, weakness, weight loss, or fever), stay as active as possible. Here are tips for how to handle back pain and activity: Stop normal physical activity for only the first few days. This helps calm your symptoms and reduce swelling (inflammation) in the area of the pain. Apply heat or ice to the painful area. Use ice for the first 48 to 72 hours, and then use heat. Take over-the-counter pain relievers such as ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin IB) or acetaminophen (Tylenol). Sleep in a curled-up, fetal position with a pillow between your legs. If you usually sleep on your back, place a pillow or rolled towel under your knees to relieve pressure.
sources.preview: A common myth about back pain is that you need to rest and avoid activity for a long time. In fact, health care providers do not recommend bed rest. If you have no sign of a serious cause for your back pain (such as loss of bowel or bladder control, weakness,

cases.output:
DIAGNOSIS:
Low back pain

WHAT WE FOUND:
Your back hurts but we did not find anything serious. Your strength is normal. You can move your legs. You do not have signs that you need more tests right now.

WHAT TO DO AT HOME:
You can use ice or heat. Both can help. Use ice for the first two or three days. Put ice on your back for about 20 minutes at a time. Do this two or three times a day. Do not put ice right on your skin. Use a towel or cloth. After two or three days you can switch to heat. Use a heating pad on low. Heat helps your muscles relax.

Rest for the first few days but do not stay in bed all day. Move around a little bit each day. Start slow. Do not do hard work or heavy lifting right away. Stop if something makes the pain worse.

When you sleep, try lying on your side with your knees pulled up. Put a pillow between your knees. If you sleep on your back, put a pillow under your knees.

MEDICATIONS:
You can take pain medicine you buy at the store.

Ibuprofen or naproxen can help with pain and swelling. Do not use ibuprofen or naproxen if you have kidney problems, if you have had stomach ulcers or bleeding, if you take blood thinners, or if your doctor told you to avoid them.

You can also use acetaminophen for pain.

Follow the directions on the bottle. Do not take more than the bottle says.

RETURN TO ED IF:
Come back to the emergency room right away if you have any of these problems:

Your legs become weak or you cannot walk.

You feel numbness in your groin, buttocks, or private area.

You cannot control your pee or poop.

You have a fever.

You fall or get hurt badly.

Your pain gets much worse even after rest and medicine.

FOLLOW UP:
See your regular doctor in one to two weeks if your pain is not better. Your doctor may send you to physical therapy or another specialist if you need more help.
